lesson.title: Network Topologies
lesson.objective: Define network topology and explain its importance
Describe the five common network topologies
Compare the advantages and disadvantages of each topology
lesson.definition: The arrangement of nodes (computers, switches, routers) and connections (cables, wireless links) in a network. It describes both the physical layout and the logical flow of data.
lesson.example: All devices connect to a single shared cable (the "bus"). Data travels along the cable and is received by all devices, but only the intended recipient processes it. Advantage: Simple, inexpensive. Disadvantage: A single cable failure breaks the entire network.

All devices connect to a central hub or switch. This is the most common topology in modern LANs (home routers, office switches). Advantage: Easy to manage, failure of one device doesn't affect others. Disadvantage: Central hub is a single point of failure.
Devices connect in a circular chain. Data travels in one direction around the ring. Advantage: Equal access for all nodes. Disadvantage: A single broken connection can disrupt the entire network.

Every device has a dedicated connection to every other device. Used in WANs and critical infrastructure. Advantage: Highly redundant, no single point of failure. Disadvantage: Expensive, complex cabling.
A combination of two or more topologies (e.g., a star-bus or star-ring). Most real-world networks are hybrid topologies.
lesson.note: For modern enterprise networks, star topology (with a central managed switch) is the standard choice for LANs. Mesh topology is used in the internet's core routing infrastructure.
lesson.diagram: Bus: A---B---C---D---E (single cable) Star: A /|\ B-S-C (S = Switch/Hub) \|/ D Ring: A--B--C--D--A (circular) Mesh: A---B |\ /| | X | (every node connected to every other) |/ \| C---D

question.text: Which topology provides the highest level of redundancy?
In a star topology, if the central switch fails, all network communication stops.

lesson.exercise: Draw a diagram of a small office network with 8 computers, 1 switch, and 1 router. What topology does this represent? What would happen if the switch failed?

summary.item: Network topology describes how devices are physically and logically connected
Bus: Simple but fragile; Star: Most common, central switch; Ring: Circular path
Mesh: Maximum redundancy, used in WANs; Hybrid: Real-world combination
Star topology dominates modern LANs; mesh is used in critical infrastructure
